title: What an American thinks about Paraguay

transcript.text: What an American thinks about Paraguay.
I was in Paraguay for a couple days, like 10 years ago.
Took a bus all the way across the city to their main, their capital city, Asuncion.
Asuncion is a fair-sized city, not really big.
In America, it'd be like a big city, but not like a, it'd be like a city, but not like a big city.
Maybe it was like, it's, you can't say how many people live there because it's different.

A city of the same size in America would be three times larger in size than, you know, like Denver is 500, is like a million people, metropolitan, but Santiago is 5 million people, but Denver is bigger than Santiago, so.
So, yeah, but I guess American terms, Asuncion would be maybe like a couple hundred thousand, it seemed like, or a hundred thousand.
We probably had more than that.
But I was there, and it was totally abandoned.
There was nobody there.
It was, you look down the streets and literally no cars.
Like, like in the middle of the city, in the middle of that day, maybe it was the weekend or Sunday or something that I was there, but you look down the city and the road and like literally no cars.
Like you could stand there for 10 minutes and there wouldn't be a car.
It was really bizarre.
It was really bizarre.
Nothing was open.
Nobody was there.
Totally abandoned.
Really weird.
But, uh, it was next to a river and, um, I guess the other side of the river was Argentina.
But I didn't see any, any towns or anything.
There weren't any really towns next to Asuncion.
It was just Asuncion.
It's not a very popular city, but they had a, um, they had an, uh, like a dictator there back in the 80s.
He wasn't very popular who got overthrown or something.
And I was standing in front of like where his palace was and the guy, there were these guys standing in front and they were saying good things about him.
Like, oh yeah, yeah, he was good.
He was good.
He did this.
He did that.
They were being friendly.
Speaking Spanish.
But, uh, that's all.
That's the only people I talked to that day.
I just walked around and then there's this park that was kind of not very populated that was next to the river.
And the, the, the bus ride across the country was really, um, I didn't see any, uh, it was just plains, really flat, not, not, not any big towns, a couple of houses I saw.
And then they had this town on the eastern side of the country that was a town that I think it was in three countries.
Half of the town was in Brazil.
Half of the, it was a town that you would stay at if you want to see Foz do Iguazu.
Those big falls, um, which is right on the border.
But I think this town was on the border too.
Half of it was in Brazil and half of it was in Paraguay.
And I stayed on the Paraguay side because I wanted to be in Paraguay.
But, um, and I thought maybe half it was in Argentina too or something like that.
The Brazilian side was bigger and the Paraguayan side was really small and it was dominated by Chinese people.
And I went into this Chinese, like, mini mall area and I wanted to get something to eat, but she said it was closed, but they looked open.
So maybe they only wanted to serve the Chinese people, but, um, yeah, that's, that's my experience of Paraguay.
